div1.entryFree: Whistle, vb. to make a shrill sound, either with the mouth or a wind instrument: As II, 7, 163. Rom. V, 3, 7. Lr. II, 2, 163. Ant. II, 2, 221. Per. IV, 1, 64. With an object: tunes . . . that he heard the carmen w. H4B III, 2, 342. Applied to the sound of winds: Mids. II, 1, 86. H4A V, 1, 5. To the call of falconers: I'ld w. her off and let her down the wind, Oth. III, 3, 262 (Johnson: the falconers always let fly the hawk against the wind; if she flies with the wind behind her, she seldom returns. If therefore a hawk was for any reason to be dismissed, she was let down the wind, and from that time shifted for herself and preyed at fortune). to w. off these secrets, Wint. IV, 4, 248 (the clown's speech, who meant to say whisper, but in his blunder says more than he intended). this being done, let the law go w. IV, 4, 715 (i. e. you are beyond its reach. Proverbial phrase).
